speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready. No. No. Whenever you're ready. You're gonna read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This interview is being conducted with Hilde Wolf Jaffe, on August 3, 1995. The interviewer is Amy Margolis Cohen. The interview is being conducted in New York, New York, in the United States, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This interview is with Hilde Wolf Jaffe on August 3, 1995. It's being conducted in New York, New York, United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English with Amy Margolis Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you please state your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilde Wolf Jaffe. That's H-I-L-D-E, W-O-L-F, J-A-F-F-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Surth, near Koln-- Cologne, in 19-- on-- on May 21, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Germany, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us a little bit about your life as a little girl in Germany before the war began?

speaker: interviewee
text: My life in Germany before the war began started out very nicely. My father had a butcher business that was fine, as far as I remember. We had-- in my house was my mother, my father, my older sister. There was a woman who was a-- slept in. She was a maid. And there was a man who worked with my father in the store. And things were pretty nicely settled. And I was very fond of Ketchen. That was the woman who worked for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-T-C-H-E-N. It's a diminutive of Kate, I suppose. But Ketchen was very important in my early life. She took care of me a lot. So our life was pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Sally, S-A-L-L-Y. And even though that is a woman's name in the United States, it's a man's name-- certainly was a man's name in Germany, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: His birthday was January 9, 1893. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was, I would say, a-- typically German in many of his attitudes in that he was a hard worker. He was very proud of the skills that he had acquired running a business and doing all the things that a butcher has to do. And he was proud, because he was a master butcher, and he was able to have apprentices. And all of this is very much controlled in Germany, certainly in the Germany of the pre-Hitler days. The-- the guilds were very powerful. And my father had all the official credentials that went with that. We had a very comfortable life. My father was very busy. He used to get up very, very early. And although he wasn't that tender loving father that's always around when you need him, he-- he didn't go anywhere to work. I could always see him either in the store or in the-- the-- in the place where-- behind the house, there was a building where they did the slaughtering, and they made German sausages. They made everything from frankfurters, to salami, and [LAUGHS] liverwurst. [LAUGHS] And my father evidently did this very well. And with the people helping him, he-- he ran a pretty efficient business. So that was what life was like.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your mother. When was she born and what was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born on January 24th. We were never quite sure when her birthday was. It was either the 22nd or the 24th. But I think, officially, it was the 24th, because that's when her birth was registered-- in-- in 1897.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Fanny, F-A-N-N-Y. Her maiden name was Baer, B-A-E-R. And the family joke was that a bear married a wolf. And that was always amusing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother was very-- also very busy always. She-- she worked in the store behind the counter. And again, although she was around all the time, she was also always busy. So my day-to-day companion, when I was a very little girl, was Ketchen. I used to watch her work. I was a, you know-- she-- she talked to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And really, one of my earliest memories was in the winter, in the snow, we had a sled, and Ketchen getting me dressed and taking me for a sleigh ride. She would pull me all over town. She was really, a pretty wonderful person. Those were some of my memories. I spent a lot of time alone, also. I remember spending time in the garden that was behind our house. We had a-- a big lilac bush and a pear tree, and a-- a little vegetable patch. And there were roses and flowers. And I remember sitting on the ground in the grass, playing, often with our dog. We had a big dog, a big Great Dane. And he was my very good friend. As a matter of fact, somewhere there's a picture of me and the Great Dane. The dog is almost bigger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was four years older. And she had mostly her own friends. I don't remember playing with her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Herta, H-E-R-T-A. She was there, but she wasn't there. She had her own friends. She went to a different school. By the time I went to school, she was in another school. So we-- we'd-- we were not very close. And unfortunately, that even carried on into our adult life. And-- but early on, I remember playing by myself a great deal. I-- I had some friends in the village, in Surth. And I had one little girlfriend who also was called Hilde, and we played together. There were no Jewish children to play with. These were all Christians. As a matter of fact, in the entire village of Surth, there were only three Jewish families, and none of them had children my age. So that made-- made a big difference in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the Jewish and the non-Jewish families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friendly. When I was a very little girl, before Hitler times, we were-- we were friends with a number of non-Jewish people. My father was born in the same house that I was born in. And they-- he had friends that he went to school with. There was always a kind of a difference, you know. There was a slight difference. The Jews somehow didn't get intimate, but we were friendly. And there was a sense of, well, we belonged there. We were part of the community. And my father belonged to the local gymnastics group, the turnverein, as it's called in Germany. He was a very athletic man. He-- he could-- he was a good swimmer. He could row a boat on the Rhine with the best of them, even when it was stormy. We had customers on the other side of the Rhine. And occasionally, he-- especially if the weather was bad-- he would deliver the meat himself. Because nobody else would take a boat across. I remember that. So he was always proud of his athletic prowess. And actually, he was that way even until he died here in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: We believed in God, but we weren't Orthodox-- not in any sense of the word. My-- we had seders and we celebrated Hanukkah. Those were the important holidays when I was a little girl. And the seder was conducted in German, because my father felt that this was the story of the Exodus for the children. And the children wouldn't understand it if he read it in Hebrew. So he always conducted the seder in the German translation. And certainly, Hanukkah was a big deal. Because on the first night, we always got lots of presents. We went to some of our Christian neighbors for Christmas, where we got more presents!

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Not bad. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but those were usually little things that the neighbors got us. I mean, the-- the big stuff we got Hanukkah from our parents. I remember getting a doll carriage. I remember getting furniture, a little table and chair set-- a wicker table and chair set. That was really very pretty. Those were Hanukkah presents. Later on, we got bicycles for Hanukkah. That was important. And we-- we knew we were Jewish all along. But we were-- what I would say, the German version of reformed Jews, even then. We went to services on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, certainly. And there was a synagogue in a neighboring town called Wesseling. I think that's spelled, W-E-S-S-E-L-I-N-G. But very often, we went to Cologne, where we worshipped at the Roonstrasse Synagogue. And that is-- was a big, liberal, reform-type temple, with an organ, and a choir, and a very impressive service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think it's spelled, R-O-H-N-S-T-A-- S-T-R-A-S-S-E--- Roonstrasse. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This synagogue, incidentally, was destroyed later on. Yeah. I remember it as a large, impressive building. But of course, the things that you remember as a child sometimes weren't quite as big [LAUGHS] or as impressive when you see them as an adult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about school.

speaker: interviewee
text: School was good and bad. They didn't know much about child psychology in those days. And my first day of school was really traumatic. It was April the 1st, 1933. And it was the day of the Jewish boycott in Germany. And my father had to close the store. The store was closed. And that was a-- a pretty terrible thing. I was very much aware of the-- the nervousness of my parents. And my mother was afraid to leave the house. So my sister took me to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it all begin when you woke up? How did you know something was wrong? What was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were saying, who is going to take me to school? The family was talking. Who is going to take me? And then it was finally decided that it would be best if my sister would go with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was explained to you what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was explained to me that this, you know, the-- the store was closed. And I remember clearly going out of the house and looking at the store window, and seeing some kind of a placard in front of the store window. And then next to the store, there was a driveway with a big door that opened up if we-- when we had trucks making deliveries. And on that big door, we called it the Tor, T-O-R. It's German for, like, a big garage door. And they-- there were placards. And it says-- said on it, kauft nicht bei Juden, which means, don't buy at a Jewish place. In other words, they were trying to tell everybody that Jews were no good. And you're not supposed to shop there. And that frightened me, because I certainly knew we were Jews. And it meant that we had enemies. I didn't even know that there were people who didn't wish us well until that time came. And that was frightening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to school. And I suppose that the teacher meant well when she met us at the door. The-- the teacher of the first graders-- because I was going into first grade. They didn't have a kindergarten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it seems like each child went into the door of the school. And the teacher was there, and took us into her-- what shall I say-- into her classroom or whatever. And as the parents delivered the child, the child was delivered to the teacher more or less. And I wasn't there with my mother. I wasn't there with my father. I was there with my sister. And the teacher recognized my sister. And my sister was a wonderful student. She always was. She was the best in everything. She had the best penmanship. She always knew all the answers. And the teacher said to me, as a greeting, hello. Oh, I see your sister is here, and I hope you'll be as good as your sister. And from that moment I was determined not to be as good as my sister. [LAUGHS] I was going to be my own self. And I must say, even though I always passed everything. I have my old report cards. It-- it always said gut, gut, gut, but it never said sehr gut, except maybe for behavior or something like that. But somehow, I never got those sehr guts. I really didn't try very hard. Because who wanted to be so good like my sister who always gets sehr good in everything-- sehr gut meaning, very good, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was my introduction to school in Germany. But by and large, the teacher, whose name was Mrs. Klumper, Frau Klumper-- and that's spelled K-L-U with an umlaut-- M-P-E-R-- was really a nice, motherly kind of person. I remember her being big and a bit stout.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she treated me all right. I-- she certainly was no Nazi, considering that I started school in 1933. And she was, I think, my teacher for the first three or four grades. I don't remember exactly when it changed. And then, I know in my very last year in school, in Germany, which was in the fifth grade, I had another teacher, a man, a Herr Mohr, M-O-H-R. And he-- he was really very nice, really-- also a wonderful person. I-- I didn't suffer from my teachers. The school itself was actually a Catholic school. It was what we call a public school and a volksschule, but it was the Katholische volksschule, which means it was a Catholic public school. It was associated with the church, and there was religious instruction in the school. And I and a few Evangelical-- or-- Protestant-- students, and there were just a few in this town where I went to school and lived-- the Protestant students and I were excused from the Catholic instruction. So we used to go out in the schoolyard and play. There was, however, one little catch. My father had told the teacher that when they do the I should stay in, because it wouldn't hurt me to learn about the And actually, that was the beginning of my religious instruction. I did stay whenever they had the

speaker: interviewee
text: So the religious instruction, I think was done-- we had a different teacher for that. So it must have been someone from the church, either the pastor or whoever they sent. How did you get to school each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked. I walked. It was a short walk, maybe 15 minutes or so. And we-- we went to school. We always had snack time around 10 o'clock. School started at 8:00 in the morning. At 10 o'clock there was a 20 minute break. And we all ate a sandwich-- when the weather was nice-- in the schoolyard. And we played for 20 minutes, and then went back. School was over at noon, at 12 o'clock. And then we went home for dinner, for, you know, a good midday meal. And that was our warm meal of the day. Then on some days, I think once or twice a week, school was in the afternoon from 2 o'clock on-- from 2:00 to 4:00-- but not every day. In the afternoon, the girls would learn how to do needlework. You learned how to sew, how to crochet, how to knit, and do embroidery. And that was what the girls learned how to do. And the boys would have some kind of, I guess, woodworking or something. We-- we were always separated in the afternoon. And then another afternoon we did-- we did gym, you know. Especially in the-- when the weather was nice, we would go to the sportplatz, which was a-- an open field, which had a track around it. It was a-- like a stadium, I suppose-- but not really a stadium. There were no spectators. But it was a place to do gymnastics, and to-- to do track, or field and track work. And-- and that was once a week we did the sports. Now later on, and this is getting a little dim in my mind, I think there were afternoon sessions for all the other kids that I wasn't included in, where they met with the Hitler Jugend. And they would-- the kids would march, and they'd wear their brown uniforms. But of course, I wasn't included in that, because I was a little Jewish girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't pick on you or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody picked on me. But I just didn't go to those things. And as I said, my-- my school was like in a cocoon. [LAUGHS] In the classroom, no one ever picked on me. The teacher stood guard if anybody tried to. And I don't remember people trying. However, the other kids were in the Hitler Jugend. And they used to march. And they used to sing those songs, you know. And I remember the songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what comes to mind? [SINGING IN GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: I know the words. [LAUGHS] What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means, the-- the flag flies high. Our ranks are closed. The SR marches on. And they would march, you know, through this-- through the town. They were Nazis. They're the leaders in those days, yes. They-- but-- the-- the but is, I think, who-- you know, there was nobody there who wanted to hurt me personally. It was, like, separated-- as far as I was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in first grade? Or was this in the later years?

speaker: interviewee
text: This developed later, I-- I think. I'm not sure when it started. It-- I think it already started in the first and second grade, you know, that these things were going on, that people were marching. They-- I remember the people were wearing uniforms, the brown uniforms, the black uniform of the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: The young kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Those were the men. Those were the men. The young kids had brown uniform-- for Hitler Jugend. You know, that was a-- a tan uniform, if I remember. And I think first it was the boys only, and then there were girls also. They marched together, and they did drills on the sportplatz, you know, the-- the place where we went for-- to do gym work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how they got recruited?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember somebody, a soldier coming and speaking to any of the teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This-- this kind of happened when I wasn't there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you saw this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scary-- very scary. I had a lot of scary-- there was a lot of things going on. It was-- this was the-- the bad thing that happened to me as a child. I remember the day the Reichstag burned. I don't remember the exact date, but I know it was somewhere before I went to school. And I remember the neighbors-- the-- not the Christian neighbors, but the other two-- the men from the other two Jewish families in town-- coming to the house and talking to my father, and saying, this isn't going to be good for the Jews. This is going to be terrible. This conversation went on before I went to sleep. Then I remember going to bed, my mother putting me to bed, and lying in bed looking at the window, and being very frightened. Because I didn't know if something was going to be happening that minute. Children don't have-- I suppose I-- I couldn't look at this in an abstract way, like something that might happen sometime in the future. I thought this-- something terrible is going to happen tonight or-- or maybe tomorrow night. And-- and I just was very frightened. I couldn't go to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was rough. And that kind of feeling became more and more pronounced. I-- I just-- even though nobody really attacked me personally as a child, there were all kinds of things going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: A new newspaper was being published. Very often the-- the pages were mounted on our door-- swastikas. The newspaper was I remember it. It-- the-- which means the-- the-- der sturmer, and storm is an-- the-- the storm, you know. I don't know exactly how to translate that. But it-- it-- I guess it had to do with storm troopers, or whatever. But that was the local newspaper-- the Nazi newspaper. And they kept on writing articles about how Jews were dirty, and how they you know, they-- they were cheating. And they were doing these horrible things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow-- could this be me, my parents? How can this be? I mean, it was just total disconnect. And as a child, I-- I was just frightened a lot by all this talk and all this business going on. What did it mean to you as a 6, or a 7, or 8-year-old? Did you understand what was really going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I really understood exactly what was going on, but I knew something was going on that was out to destroy us. Something was there that was terrible. And the-- the worst thing was that my parents were frightened. They acted frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they act frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was kind of a-- the-- the kind of person who would get an upset stomach if something terrible would happen. And she walked around with an upset stomach a lot. And my father tried to be calm and reassure her. But even his reassurance, I mean, it-- it was disturbing. I mean, there were things going on that I didn't like. There was a lot of talk about my sister going to the next school. Normally, she-- she would, as she did, have started out in the volksschule, which was a local school. But then in the fourth grade, she would have gone to a gymnasium.
